narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 37, you've clearly put in the effort to build a strong academic foundation, including reaching the 7th decile in your school's Year 3 grades and coming from a middle-income family background. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 64%, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out as the strongest influences. The most significant factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your choice to attend part-time, which is uncommon—only about 7% of students do so—and the model sees it as a major hurdle compared to the typical full-time path taken by over 92% of applicants. On a positive note, your strong Year 3 decile ranking worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other positive element, as it shows clear improvement over time. Your Year 1 decile of 4th place had a moderate drag, suggesting an early weaker start relative to peers, though your LSAT score helped nudge things positively by being slightly above average. Your GPA and family income played smaller roles, with the GPA slightly tilting against you and income having minimal impact. Overall, compared to typical students who often start stronger in early years and go full-time, your profile signals higher risk to the model despite your later gains. While this is disappointing, it's not the end of the road—consider reapplying after bolstering your early academic record through additional coursework or exploring full-time options to align better with successful patterns. You might also retake the LSAT to push that score higher or seek programs that value part-time trajectories. We're rooting for your success, and with targeted improvements, you can shift this prediction in your favor next time. Keep pushing forward.
